The Dormouse is a small, perpetually drowsy creature who appears as a guest at the Mad Tea-Party (Chapter 7), seated between the March Hare and the Hatter, and later attends the trial in court (Chapter 11) arm-in-arm with the March Hare. It serves as a sleepy, easily-bullied minor companion to the tea-party trio.

[FACTS]
- The Dormouse sat between the March Hare and the Hatter, fast asleep, during the tea-party.
- The March Hare and the Hatter used the sleeping Dormouse as a cushion, resting their elbows on it.
- The Hatter poured hot tea on the Dormouse's nose to wake or quiet it.
- The March Hare and the Hatter pinched it on both sides at once to wake it.
- The Dormouse began telling a story in a great hurry when asked.
- The tea-party companions tried to put the Dormouse into the teapot.
- At the trial it attended court arm-in-arm with the March Hare and gave testimony ("Sixteenth").
- It told Alice she had "no right to grow here" while sitting next to her.
- The Queen shrieked "Collar that Dormouse! Behead that Dormouse!" causing confusion as it was turned out.
- It spoke "Treacle" in a sleepy voice.

[INFERENCES]
- The Dormouse is the lowest-status member of the tea-party, tolerated and abused by the others. [conf:高]
- It is chronically sleepy and struggles to stay awake during interactions. [conf:高]
- It is timid and easily irritated rather than assertive, growing sulky and indignant when provoked. [conf:中]
- It is socially aligned with the March Hare and Hatter, traveling and appearing with them. [conf:中]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The Dormouse is reactive rather than deliberate, responding only when prodded, pinched, or poured upon, and otherwise lapsing instantly back to sleep. Its personality is drowsy, feeble, and prone to sulkiness and indignation when disturbed, yet it can be peevish toward Alice ("you've no right to grow here"). It takes no clear stance on the story's themes of order and nonsense, functioning instead as a passive object of others' whims and authority. Its characteristic habits are a hoarse, feeble, sleepy voice, half-asleep mutterings like "Treacle" and "Twinkle, twinkle," and a tendency to be physically manipulated by those around it.

[GAPS]
- The text never reveals the Dormouse's gender consistently (referred to as "it" and "his").
- We learn nothing of its origins or how it joined the tea-party.
- The full content and ending of its interrupted story are never given.

The Hatter is one of the mad inhabitants of Wonderland whom the Cheshire Cat directs Alice toward. He hosts the perpetual tea-party alongside the March Hare and the Dormouse, and is later summoned as the first witness at the Knave of Hearts' trial.

[FACTS]
- The Cheshire Cat describes the Hatter as living nearby and as mad.
- The Hatter quarrelled with Time last March at a concert given by the Queen of Hearts, where he had to sing "Twinkle, twinkle, little bat!"
- The Queen declared he was "murdering the time" and called for his head to be cut off.
- He poses a riddle to Alice and asks whether she has guessed it.
- He poured hot tea on the Dormouse's nose and, with the March Hare, pinched the Dormouse and tried to put it into the teapot.
- He demanded a clean cup and announced everyone should move one place on.
- He was rude to Alice, prompting her to leave in disgust.
- He was the first witness called at the trial, arriving with a teacup and bread-and-butter.
- During testimony he turned pale, trembled, shook off his shoes, and bit a piece out of his teacup.
- He told the King the hat he wore was not his own and that he could not go lower as he was on the floor.

[INFERENCES]
- The Hatter is genuinely terrified of the Queen and King's authority. [conf:高]
- His relationship with Time is treated as literal/personified, suggesting Wonderland's surreal logic. [conf:中]
- He is socially careless and provocative, prioritizing wit and retort over courtesy. [conf:中]
- He may earn his living dealing in hats that are not personally his. [conf:低]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The Hatter acts impulsively and reactively, firing off retorts and abrupt commands ("move one place on," "I want a clean cup") rather than reflecting on others' feelings. His personality is sharp-tongued, contemptuous, and triumphant when scoring a point, yet he collapses into trembling nervousness under genuine authority. He embodies the work's themes of madness, nonsensical logic, and the tyranny of arbitrary power, having literally fallen foul of "Time" and the Queen's death-sentences. His characteristic habits include riddling, mistreating the sleepy Dormouse, and carrying his tea-things everywhere—biting his teacup and dropping his bread-and-butter when frightened.

[GAPS]
- The text never reveals the Hatter's real name or origin.

- The reason the tea-party is perpetually stuck at tea-time is only hinted at, not fully explained.

The Caterpillar is a large blue caterpillar Alice encounters sitting atop a mushroom while smoking a hookah, in the fourth and fifth chapters. It serves as an enigmatic interlocutor who tests Alice's recitations and ultimately provides her the means to control her size via the mushroom.

[FACTS]
- The Caterpillar is a large blue caterpillar that sits on top of a mushroom with its arms folded, quietly smoking a long hookah.
- It addressed Alice in a languid, sleepy voice and made very short remarks.
- It told Alice her recitation was "not said right" and judged a recitation "wrong from beginning to end."
- It instructed Alice to repeat "You are old, Father William."
- It asked Alice "What size do you want to be?" and reacted angrily when she mentioned three inches.
- It declared three inches "a very good height indeed," speaking angrily.
- It is itself exactly three inches high.
- It explained that one side of the mushroom makes you grow taller and the other makes you grow shorter.
- It got down off the mushroom and crawled away in the grass.

[INFERENCES]
- The Caterpillar is irritable and easily provoked, especially regarding the topic of size. [conf:高]
- The Caterpillar's anger about three inches stems from it being its own height. [conf:中]
- The Caterpillar holds knowledge useful to Alice that it dispenses sparingly and unprompted assistance. [conf:中]
- The Caterpillar regards correct recitation/order as important, acting as a kind of examiner. [conf:中]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The Caterpillar acts with deliberate detachment, speaking slowly and answering in clipped, minimal remarks rather than rushing to engage. Its personality is languid and indifferent yet sharply critical and prone to sudden anger, particularly when its own height of three inches is implicitly slighted. Toward the work's recurring theme of identity and bodily transformation, it functions as a gatekeeper, treating questions of size as serious and offering the mushroom as the mechanism for change. Its characteristic habits are smoking a long hookah while seated atop the mushroom with arms folded, correcting Alice's flawed recitations, and finally crawling away into the grass without ceremony.

[GAPS]
- The source does not reveal the Caterpillar's name, origin, or why it sits on the mushroom.
- It is unstated why the Caterpillar smokes a hookah or what significance it holds.
- The text does not say whether the Caterpillar will transform (as caterpillars do) or appear again.

entities.canonical_name: a crowd of little animals and birds
entities.note_md:
[CONTEXT]
A loosely-defined collective of small animals and birds that appears alongside Alice in the early chapters, beginning when several fall into the pool of tears. They function as a group character—swimming, racing, and later assembling—rather than as distinct named individuals.

[FACTS]
- The crowd includes a Duck, a Dodo, a Lory, an Eaglet, and several other curious creatures.
- The birds and animals fell into the pool of tears, which became crowded with them.
- The whole party swam to the shore together.
- After a race, the small ones could not taste their comfits, choked, and had to be patted on the back.
- A crowd of little animals and birds waited outside the house, including the Lizard named Bill.
- They all made a rush at Alice the moment she appeared.
- A great crowd of all sorts of small creatures assembled at the throne scene.

[INFERENCES]
- The group is recurring background presence across multiple chapters rather than a one-time appearance. [conf:中]
- The crowd tends to act collectively and impulsively as a mass (swimming together, rushing at Alice). [conf:中]
- The "small ones" are physically vulnerable, given they choked and needed help. [conf:低]
- The crowd's gathering at the house and throne suggests they follow events and congregate around moments of spectacle. [conf:低]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
The crowd acts as a single impulsive mass, moving and reacting in unison without apparent deliberation—swimming to shore together, choking together, and rushing at Alice the instant she appears. Its collective temperament is excitable and curious, drawn to assemble around any commotion or central figure. The group has no articulated stance on the work's themes; it serves as a reactive chorus to Alice's adventures rather than a deliberate agent. The little door is a tiny passage discovered behind a low curtain in the great hall, standing about fifteen inches high and leading into a beautiful garden. It serves as Alice's recurring goal in the early chapters, an obstacle of scale she must repeatedly negotiate by changing her size.

- Alice opened the door and could see the garden beyond. (ch01_p001)
- The door was later found shut again, with the key out of reach. (ch02_p001)
- It was located in the great hall along with the glass table. (ch04_p000)
- The door opens inwards. (ch04_p001)
- Alice eventually unlocked it with the golden key and got through once small enough. (ch04_p002, ch07_p003)
- A separate door figures in other scenes where Alice and others knock, open, or stand near it. (ch06_p000, ch11_p002)

[INFERENCES]

[CONTEXT]
The threat of execution—usually beheading—is a recurring instrument of intimidation in the royal court, invoked by the Queen and the King to assert authority. It appears in the garden among the gardeners and later in the courtroom during the Hatter's testimony.

[FACTS]
- The Queen is said to have declared someone "deserved to be beheaded" only the day before.
- The court inhabitants are described as "dreadfully fond of beheading people."
- The gardeners fear that if the Queen finds something out, they "should all have our heads cut off."
- The King threatens to have the witness "executed on the spot" if he is nervous.
- The threat of execution is used to pressure the Hatter to give evidence.

[INFERENCES]

- The threat is applied arbitrarily and disproportionately, often for trivial or non-existent offenses. [conf:中]

[CONTEXT]
"Growing larger and smaller" refers to the recurring phenomenon by which Alice's body repeatedly changes size throughout her adventures, often triggered by eating or nibbling various items. It surfaces across early chapters as a destabilizing feature of the world she has fallen into, and is discussed directly in her exchange with the Caterpillar.

[FACTS]
- Alice undergoes the changes many times in a single day.
- The changes are triggered by nibbling, e.g. at the mushroom — first at one side, then the other — making her sometimes taller and sometimes shorter.
- Alice states she does not keep the same size for ten minutes together.
- Alice describes being "so many different sizes in a day" as very confusing.
- The Caterpillar disputes Alice's claim that the changes are confusing, saying "It isn't."
- Alice complains she is "never sure what I'm going to be, from one minute to another."

[INFERENCES]
- The size changes are largely involuntary or unpredictable from Alice's perspective. [conf:中]
- Some of the changes can be deliberately controlled through measured nibbling of the mushroom. [conf:中]
- The phenomenon contributes to Alice's broader loss of stable identity in this world. [conf:中]
- Different characters do not share Alice's sense that the changes are strange or troubling. [conf:低]

[BEHAVIOR_PATTERN]
- (not applicable for non-Agent entities)

[GAPS]
- The text here does not fully explain what mechanisms (foods, drinks, objects) cause each specific change.
- It is unclear whether Alice ever gains reliable control over the changes.
- The physical limits or extremes of the size changes are not stated.
- Whether the changes have lasting consequences beyond confusion is not recorded.

"The Knave stole the tarts" is the central accusation at the trial scene, drawn from the verse read aloud declaring the Knave of Hearts took the tarts away. It functions as the disputed claim of guilt around which the courtroom argument turns.

[FACTS]
